body:
in Art, History |   	March 2nd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

?si=HOvgnTtB4xSNqpNE
In 1894, archae┬şol┬şo┬şgist ├ëdouard Piette dis┬şcov┬şered the ÔÇťVenus of Brassem┬şpouy,ÔÇŁ oth┬şer┬şwise known as the ÔÇťLady with the Hood.ÔÇŁ Unearthed in south┬şwest┬şern France and dat┬şing to around 25,000┬áBCE, this carv┬şing rep┬şre┬şsents the ear┬şli┬şest real┬şis┬ştic depic┬ştion of a human face. The figureÔÇÖs fore┬şhead, nose, and brows are care┬şful┬şly carved in relief, as is the hair, arranged in a neat geo┬şmet┬şric pat┬ştern. But what hap┬şpened to the mouth? Or the eyes? WeÔÇÖre not sure.
The Venus is carved from mam┬şmoth ivory, like┬şly using a stone flint, and stands just 3.65┬ácm tall. For some, it marks a major devel┬şop┬şment in fig┬şu┬şra┬ştive art. Or, as his┬şto┬şri┬şan Simon Schama has sug┬şgest┬şed, this fig┬şurine may well be the ÔÇťdawn of the idea of beau┬ştyÔÇŁ in human cul┬şture.

The WorldÔÇÖs Old┬şest Cave Art, Dis┬şcov┬şered in Indone┬şsia, Is at Least 67,800 Years Old

title: The Greatest Double Agent Ever: How a Spanish Chicken Farmer Became the Most Important Double Agent in WWII
description:
in History |   	March 2nd, 2026  1 Comment
	
			

	

Juan Pujol Gar┬şc├şa was one of the rare indi┬şvid┬şu┬şals whose par┬ştic┬şi┬şpa┬ştion in World War II m
body:
in History |   	March 2nd, 2026  1 Comment
	
			

	

Juan Pujol Gar┬şc├şa was one of the rare indi┬şvid┬şu┬şals whose par┬ştic┬şi┬şpa┬ştion in World War II made him a Mem┬şber of the Order of the British Empire and earned him the Iron Cross. He gained that unlike┬şly dis┬ştinc┬ştion in per┬şhaps the riski┬şest of all roles in espi┬şonage, that of a dou┬şble agent. Despite ulti┬şmate┬şly work┬şing for the Allied cause, he cre┬şat┬şed an elab┬şo┬şrate fic┬ştion┬şal per┬şsona ÔÇö com┬şplete with an invent┬şed spy net┬şwork oper┬şat┬şing across Great Britain ÔÇö who pro┬şfessed loy┬şal┬şty to the Nazi cause. Not only did Pujol get this char┬şac┬şter plugged into the real Ger┬şman intel┬şli┬şgence sys┬ştem, he also got him on its pay┬şroll, receiv┬şing what came to the equiv┬şa┬şlent of more than $6 mil┬şlion in todayÔÇÖs U.S. dol┬şlars for sup┬şply┬şing infor┬şma┬ştion ÔÇö infor┬şma┬ştion that ulti┬şmate┬şly con┬ştributed to the AxisÔÇÖ loss of the war.
The sto┬şry of how this chick┬şen farmer from Barcelona became the most impor┬ştant dou┬şble agent of World War II is told in the ani┬şmat┬şed Pri┬şmal Space video above. Unlike many of the spies his┬şto┬şry has remem┬şbered more clear┬şly, Pujol did┬şnÔÇÖt begin his espi┬şonage career in the employ of any gov┬şern┬şment in par┬ştic┬şu┬şlar.
Rad┬şi┬şcal┬şized, if that be the word, by the expe┬şri┬şence of hav┬şing been draft┬şed into the Span┬şish Civ┬şil War, he vowed to ded┬şi┬şcate his life to ÔÇťthe good of human┬şi┬şty.ÔÇŁ Turned away by the British embassy, to which heÔÇÖd offered his ser┬şvices because Britain opposed Nazi Ger┬şmany, he went free┬şlance, re-invent┬şing him┬şself as a Third Reich-loy┬şal Span┬şish mil┬şi┬ştary man seek┬şing an assign┬şment in the U.K. Tak┬şen on by Ger┬şmany, he instead decamped to Lis┬şbon, where he began man┬şu┬şfac┬ştur┬şing ersatz intel┬şli┬şgence reports using news┬şreel footage and tourist brochures.
How┬şev┬şer makeshift, PujolÔÇÖs craft proved impres┬şsive to both Ger┬şmany and Britain, which launched an inter┬şna┬ştion┬şal spy hunt for him. He thus accom┬şplished his goal of becom┬şing an offi┬şcial British dou┬şble agent, in which capac┬şi┬şty he arrived at his finest hour: mis┬şlead┬şing the Ger┬şmans as to the 1944 ÔÇťDÔÇĹDayÔÇŁ inva┬şsion of Nor┬şmandy in an effort called Oper┬şa┬ştion For┬şti┬ştude. In Span┬şish, that would be For┬şt┬şaleza, which became the title of an RTVE doc┬şu┬şmen┬ştary about PujolÔÇÖs long-untold sto┬şry a few years ago. But if any sin┬şgle word reflects PujolÔÇÖs con┬ştri┬şbu┬ştion to his┬şto┬şry, that word must be Gar┬şbo, the code name assigned him by his first British case offi┬şcer. After all, what oth┬şer name ÔÇö at least in 1942 ÔÇö could quite so evoca┬ştive┬şly befit an agent whose skills of craft┬şing and inhab┬şit┬şing invent┬şed char┬şac┬şters made his han┬şdlers regard him as ÔÇťthe best actor in the worldÔÇŁ?
Relat┬şed con┬ştent:
The CIAÔÇÖs For┬şmer Chief of Dis┬şguise Show How Spies Use Cos┬ştumes in Under┬şcov┬şer Oper┬şa┬ştions
The Sto┬şry of Elize┬şbeth Fried┬şman, the Pio┬şneer┬şing Cryp┬ştol┬şo┬şgist Who Thwart┬şed the Nazis & Got Burned by J. Edgar Hoover
The French Designed a Fake Repli┬şca of Paris to Fool Ger┬şman Bombers Dur┬şing World War I
Dis┬şcov┬şer the CIAÔÇÖs Sim┬şple Sab┬şo┬ştage Field Man┬şu┬şal: A Time┬şless Guide to Sub┬şvert┬şing Any Orga┬şni┬şza┬ştion with ÔÇťPur┬şpose┬şful Stu┬şpid┬şi┬ştyÔÇŁ (1944)
The CIAÔÇÖs Rec┬ştal Tool Kit for Spies ÔÇö Cre┬şat┬şed for Tru┬şly Des┬şper┬şate Sit┬şu┬şa┬ştions Dur┬şing The Cold War
Based in Seoul,┬áCol┬şin┬áMarshall┬áwrites and broad┬şcasts on cities, lan┬şguage, and cul┬şture. HeÔÇÖs the author of the newslet┬şter┬áBooks on Cities┬áas well as the books┬áÝĽťŕÁş ýÜöýĽŻ ŕŞłýžÇ┬á(No Sum┬şma┬şriz┬şing Korea) and┬áKore┬şan Newtro.┬áFol┬şlow him on the social net┬şwork for┬şmer┬şly known as Twit┬şter at┬á@colinmarshall.

title: Radioposter Launches Paper-fi: Analog Books with Synchronized Soundtracks
description: For all the talk about 2026 being the year of analog, most of the conversation has been about rediscovering old formats like vinyl, film photograp
body:
For all the talk about 2026 being the year of analog, most of the conversation has been about rediscovering old formats like vinyl, film photography, and journaling. WhatÔÇÖs been missing from these hobbies is something genuinely new, a reason to believe paper can do more than it did before. Radioposter is betting it can.┬á
The Midwest-based startup has built what it calls Paper-fi: physical books with synchronized audio soundtracks that follow readers in real time as they turn each page. No chips embedded in the paper, no QR codes to scan. The system uses patented computer vision and other modes through a smartphone or smart glasses to track your place in the book and play the corresponding audio. Whether music, ambient sound, or narration, the soundtrack moves with you at your own pace. The books themselves are designed more like art objects than traditional reading material, showcasing highly visual stories where audio and imagery carry the narrative together. Think coffee table book meets documentary or movie score.┬á

Sound design, composition, illustration, photography, and storytelling all collapse into a single physical object. ItÔÇÖs not an audiobook. ItÔÇÖs not a picture book with a playlist. ItÔÇÖs a format that didnÔÇÖt exist before, one in which image, sequence, and sound interact on paper in ways that screens have made us forget.
After years of infinite scroll and algorithmically generated content, people are looking for more tangible hobbies. The appetite for objects you can hold, experiences that have edges and endings, is real. Radioposter is positioning Paper-fi as a way to give the medium new superpowers, making analog competitive again without abandoning what makes internet storytelling compelling.
The company publishes its own titles and the app that makes it all work. Two books have launched already, with more in development. They are also actively looking for artists who want to pitch their work for the format and have programs to support publishing end-to-end.
Whether Paper-fi becomes a lasting medium or a beautiful experiment, it represents something rare: a new analog artifact built for this moment, not borrowed from the past.
Learn more at radioposter.com.

schema.headline: Radioposter Launches Paper-fi: Analog Books with Synchronized Soundtracks

title: Tshepiso Moropa Splices Setswana Folklore and Dreams into Archival Collages
description: Using personal and archival images, Tshepiso Moropa cuts and splices delicate collages that consider the ever-evolving nature of the stories we te
body:
Using personal and archival images, Tshepiso Moropa cuts and splices delicate collages that consider the ever-evolving nature of the stories we tell. The self-taught artist draws on her background in psychology and linguistics as she plumbs African archives and oral histories, reinterpreting her findings through minimal, yet weighty compositions.
Moropa often grounds her works within dinaane and ditoro, Setswana lore and dreams, respectively. ÔÇťEach folktale carries a unique blend of history, cultural values, and human experience, serving as a wellspring of inspiration,ÔÇŁ she says in a statement. ÔÇťMy work is informed by the timeless wisdom, moral lessons, and magical elements found within Sestwana stories.ÔÇŁ
ÔÇťThe JoyÔÇŁ (2023), digital collage on archival matte, 42 x 29.7 centimeters
Fusing digital and analog techniques, the artist assembles surreal scenes with precise lines and ample negative space. Birds figure prominently and serve as spiritual guides as in pieces like ÔÇťThe Joy,ÔÇŁ which the artist describes as referencing the following story: ÔÇťWhen the girl drifted into the darkness, the ancestors came to her disguised as birds, their wings turning into gentle threads that lifted her away from harm.ÔÇŁ
Other works are more personal, including ÔÇťHiding in Plain Sight.ÔÇŁ Depicting an older woman floating above a wooden home, the piece alludes to one of MoropaÔÇÖs dreams in which her grandmother appeared in the sky, hovering above the artist and her twin sister.
Moropa is based in Johannesburg and represented by THK Gallery in Cape Town. This April, her work will go on view in a group exhibition at Museum Rietberg in Zurich, followed by a presentation at the Biel/Bienne Festival of Photography in Switzerland. Keep up with her practice on Instagram.
ÔÇťHiding in Plain SightÔÇŁ (2025, collage matte paper, 40 x 40 centimeters
ÔÇťRiding ShotgunÔÇŁ (2025), collage, 40 x 40 centimeters
ÔÇťThe Flying GirlÔÇŁ (2023), collage, 40 x 40 centimeters
ÔÇťThe Ride HomeÔÇŁ (2024), Digital print on Hanhem├╝hle Photo Rag, 40 x 40 centimeters

schema.headline: Tshepiso Moropa Splices Setswana Folklore and Dreams into Archival Collages

title: Maximum's Upcycled 'Billex' Process Turns Discarded Bank Notes into Furniture
description: Money manufacturers the world over are forever contending with counterfeiters. Before the U.K. introduced a new pound coin in 2017, for example, t
body:
Money manufacturers the world over are forever contending with counterfeiters. Before the U.K. introduced a new pound coin in 2017, for example, the earlier version was easy enough to fake that there were tens of millions of fraudulent copies in circulation. The same goes for paper bank notes, which over the years have been printed with increasingly high-tech features such as holograms, watermarks, and distinctive material blends. More recently, many countries have also implemented plastic coatings.
Banque de France, a central money-producing outfit in Europe, has adopted a technology called EverFit, which includes a polymer coating on cotton-blend notes that increases durability. Every year, up to three billion notes may be printed, but they must adhere to the strictest standards of technical quality. If they donÔÇÖt, theyÔÇÖre shredded. One drawback, though, is that theyÔÇÖre not recyclable. ThatÔÇÖs where Paris-based furniture design studio Maximum saw a unique opportunity.

MaximumÔÇÖs unique line of stools, called Billex, repurposes the masses of discarded bills into modern, functional objects. With a cotton substrate and two layers of plastic coating, the tiny fragments can be manipulated with heat and compressed into a hard shape. Not only is the stool a useful and stylish design piece unto itself, produced in a variety of colors, but the Billex concept is also something of a prototype. It can be employed for a wide range of applications, as the shredded notesÔÇöand the stools themselvesÔÇöcan be upcycled and compressed into virtually any shape.
See more on MaximumÔÇÖs Instagram.

schema.headline: Maximum's Upcycled 'Billex' Process Turns Discarded Bank Notes into Furniture

title: Ruby Silvious' Tiny Paintings and Assemblages Reimagine Used Teabags
description: From diminutive pieces of fine mesh, Ruby Silvious meticulously conjoins, stains, tears, and sculpts a range mixed-media collages and tiny paintin
body:
From diminutive pieces of fine mesh, Ruby Silvious meticulously conjoins, stains, tears, and sculpts a range mixed-media collages and tiny paintings from repurposed teabags. She often incorporates other elements, such as sardine tins, thread spools, and cut paper elements. The bags are emptied and flattened and sometimes connected to form longer surfaces, often keeping the strings and tabs attached. Other times, Silvious further manipulates the soft paper substrate, creating life-size garments that possess an ethereal, even spectral quality.
This spring, SilviousÔÇÖ work can be seen in group shows in Vermont, Maryland, Philadelphia, Massachusetts, and New York. During the summer, she will be collaborating with M├ílaga-based artist David Seaton, whose abstract paintings inspired some of SilviousÔÇÖ recent paper pieces, followed by a show of both of their work in Rhonda, Spain. And in the fall, her pieces will also be included in a group exhibition at Gallery of Hermosa. See more and follow updates on Instagram.

schema.headline: Ruby Silvious' Tiny Paintings and Assemblages Reimagine Used Teabags

title: Dive into Wool Creature Lab's World of Vibrant Felted Nudibranchs
description: Among the myriad delights of the marine world, nudibranchs count among some of the most adorable. There are around 3,000 known species of these of
body:
Among the myriad delights of the marine world, nudibranchs count among some of the most adorable. There are around 3,000 known species of these often very colorful, textured, soft-bodied animals. Technically part of the mollusc family, they shed their shells as they grow older, so we sometimes refer to them as ÔÇťsea slugs,ÔÇŁ but the name doesnÔÇÖt exactly live up to their inherent style. For artist Arino Borevich of Wool Creature Lab, however, these unique minuscule beings truly shine in vibrant, felted fiber.
A decade ago, Borevich was working as a cook at a remote biology research station in northern RussiaÔÇÖs White Sea. ÔÇťI was surrounded by 200 marine biologists and students living and working together on a small island,ÔÇŁ she tells Colossal. ÔÇťThat summer changed everything. It was there that I first learned about nudibranchsÔÇöthese impossibly colorful sea slugs with shapes and patterns that looked like they came from another planet.ÔÇŁ

Wool Creature Lab was born when, as a way to pass time and explore her creativity, Borevich felted a few nudibranchs to gift to some of the scientists who were studying them. She started an Etsy shop and Instagram, going with a gut feeling that these little woolen specimens ÔÇťwanted to exist in the world,ÔÇŁ she says. Soon, marine biologists and divers from all over the world began to find her work, some of whom requested particular species, including scientists who had documented certain nudibranchs for the first time.
So far, Borevich has recreated more than 40 different kinds, carefully reimagining real, scientifically described creatures into meticulously crafted fiber versions. She has an ambitious dream to sculpt every one of the thousands of known nudibranchs. ÔÇťEach sculpture takes six to 12 hours to make, so this might take a while,ÔÇŁ she says.
More than two dozen of BorevichÔÇÖs pieces were on view recently during the Xiamen International Art Festival in China as part of the Ark Farm eco-art exhibition. Support her work on Patreon, where she gives away a handmade nudibranch each month.

schema.headline: Dive into Wool Creature Lab's World of Vibrant Felted Nudibranchs
